speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Rachel Huber. It's the 28th of May, 1996. Rachel, when you arrived in Israel, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived and went into a form of absorption. At that time, that was called [NON-ENGLISH]. That means in between. These were tents with-- it was shocking, really, a shocking way of-- well, so many people came that Israel didn't have a way of absorbing that was-- that was-- there were 10 months after the establishment of the state of Israel. The absorption center, so to speak, was Tyre. That is not very far from Haifa. And what we decided was to leave and to go into Haifa. Shmuelek and I went into Haifa, look for a-- the simplest of accommodation, the most primitive of accommodation. And pay whatever we could pay. And start a new life, not sitting and waiting for somebody to give us anything. And we-- we found an accommodation, which was very simple. Shmuelek started to go to work. My father worked. My mother worked. I worked. We started a normal life. I must say that the first two years in Israel, I was exceedingly happy. We didn't have anything. But somehow the exhilaration of the new life in Israel, of the free life, of building our own life, our own home, was indescribable. My breakfast used to be watermelon with bread. And going to work, and there was-- two years, there was no meat at all to eat. There were only, for Rosh Hashanah, 100 gram of meat or whatever. But never did we feel that we want to complain, or-- or whatever. We were very, very happy. Very, very happy to be. I said 'we were' because of my-- I must justify my being now in Australia and not in Israel. Somehow, with my introduction of being so happy, it wouldn't-- the conclusion wouldn't be that I am now in-- in Australia. In 1956, there were-- seven years after we arrived in Israel, there was the Suez war. And by that time, I already had a-- a daughter of several months. The arrival of our daughter brought a evolution in-- in our life. Certainly, a first child. But particularly in my husband, having lost the whole family, and having nobody in the world, and suddenly having that extension of himself make him so emotional and so fearful of-- it might something happened to that last, last thing that he finally has in his life that means a family. And the war and the-- the whole atmosphere of the tension, the bombardment of-- of Haifa, everything, that brought in my husband a colossal insecurity. And one day, he just told me, I can't anymore. I can't take the tension. I can't. I must live somewhere where it's quiet and where I-- nothing will happen to my family. I can't stand losing my family anymore. And that was the beginning of-- of leaving Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your little girl at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Australia, she was two years old. She was-- until we came to the conclusion that we will do it, we will go to Australia, and until my parents said that they were going with us-- because always, I schlepped them always with me. And until documents came, and so-- that took time. Until we arrived in Australia, my elder daughter was two years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your elder daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Smira. That's a combination of two name, of Zahava and Miriam. Zahava was the name of my late mother-in-law. And Miriam was the name of my sister-in-law that I never knew, that was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, and not knowing if we will ever have another daughter, the dearest people that my husband lost, he made amalgam from two names and made one name, Smira. That-- basically, Smira with a S is a Arab name. But it is Zmira with a Z, common because of Zahava.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there is a name, Zamir is a nightingale in-- in Hebrew. So somehow we combined something. We combined.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: What. I think it was in July, June or July, 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had another daughter a few years, three years, I think, after arriving in Australia. I have another daughter. Her name is Deborah. She was born in 1960. I've got two lovely daughters. I've got three gorgeous grandchildren. Gorgeous grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are-- what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam is the eldest. Jonathan, the middle. And David is the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you arrived in Australia, what-- what sort of work have you and Shmuel done since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Shmuelek-- arriving in Australia, Shmuelek took the first job that came along, that was a job in a factory, and started working in a factory. I really couldn't go to work because my Smira was constantly sick. She-- her-- the acclimatization-- the change of climates-- had a reaction on her that she was constantly, constantly sick. There was no way that I could leave my girl with anything-- with anyone. And when she was already a bit acclimatized a bit better, I fell pregnant with Debbie. That took care of-- of my work in-- in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- we-- which-- at the beginning we lived together for a few months. And then we paid key money and we took a flat for-- on our own. And my parents had basically a good evening of their life here. It was quiet. It was no war, no bombs, nobody running, nobody chasing us. So they got peace here. They had-- my mother had here a brother and a sister. So she had a bit of family here. And she-- they had us. They were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has Australia been good to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Australia has been very good to me. Very good. I appreciate the life here. Appreciate-- I appreciate the peace. I appreciate the hospitality. The people were always very nice. More than anything, I appreciate the quietness here. So good to be so far away from all that the world has, with wars and famine and hating each other. It's so good to be down under. Really. I must confess that, at times, I've got a terrible guilt feeling of leaving Israel. A feeling that really the lesson of the war I really didn't carry out, in spite of my attempts to do something, I have not carried out. But there are limitations in strengths how much one can-- Maybe I-- maybe I could have stood up better. But my husband could not stood up to the continuous pressure in Israel. And I am-- feel guilty that I'm not there. But I'm grateful to Australia for the peace and the quiet that I have here, for the good life that I've got here. I had 39 quiet, happy years. I'm very grateful for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Rachel, I want to thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Renee Herscher, as I was at that time, in high school, most probably being 10 or 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, where was this-- where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Czernowitz. My high school was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And that's how I looked then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a picture taken in 1946, of myself and I-- my future husband, my later husband, my husband-to-be at that time. In displaced camp-- displaced person camp Fohrenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the time that it was taken exactly? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946. Don't know when exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, what is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document was given to us while still on the Exodus, when the English forced us to disembark from the Exodus. They gave to everyone that particular instruction-- that we should leave everything that-- whatever one had, their rucksack or whatever, to leave it there. That was written in several languages, to leave our things and to disembark. And-- and here, this-- they-- it was written that they'd take us to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You may be?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling. Oh, that's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll be OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, tell me about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the certificate that was issued to all the people that were participating in Exodus, in order to make sure that no other person will have the benefits for what we hoped would be entitled the-- the people that were in-- I was number 1,253. We didn't give any names on that, just in case it will fall to-- you see that was on-- issued on the 26th of November, 1947. With a photo, number, and-- and stamp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This particular certificate remained--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that is--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in your possession from that time on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a time, I cannot recollect the-- the place, where we were supposed to give that in. But I was-- I was working in the secretariat of the-- of the Exodus. And I wanted to have one for myself forever, to-- to have with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made myself one. The same-- my number and so on, so I should have one with me. So that's just a duplicate of another one that I gave in. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel, tell me about this picture. Yes. That is my wedding photo. That is on the 31st of March, 1950. I married my husband now. And that is-- that was in Haifa. And was a happy day for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that photo was taken in Haifa in 1955, showing my parents, my husband, and myself. That was a time when I expected my eldest daughter. I look very happy. I was happy there, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, tell me about these pictures here.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the graduation pictures of my two daughters. To the left is Smira, graduating in medicine in 1979. And to my right is Debbie, graduating in 1981 as a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That were very happy days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the bar mitzvah, a picture of my first grandson Adam. That was last year, that means '90-- '95? '95?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It is the whole family that I've got here in-- in Australia. That's my elder daughter, her husband, and two sons. My younger daughter, her husband, and their son, David. And certainly, my husband and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, are you going to do the introduction?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Shmuel, my husband of, what, 47 years? We know each other 50 years or more? Hopefully, hopefully, long, long future ahead for us, a long, long time together. I hope so. We are very happy. I'm very happy. And I'm very grateful to God that after those hard years we won't forget about it and our good years, 50 good years we have. I've got a lovely family. And I'm really, really grateful to God for everything that he's blessed me. I must [SIGHS] tell one more little thing. When we were in Poland, waiting to go, a lot of things were dug out. People were digging out. Some have left jewelry and-- or some have left silverware or whatever. And people were digging and so on. Some were happy to find letters that were-- that were written in the ghettos of Vilna or Riga. And these were written by-- this letter I have in mind was a letter that was written to one of the leaders of one of the rebellions. I do not know if it was Vilna or Riga. It was a young woman and her name was [INAUDIBLE]. She wrote a letter in which she asked everyone that survives and happens to read her letter, or has-- or happens to hear about her letter, to remember and not to let die what they went through, the heroism in trying to defend themselves and they-- they had very little ways and-- and-- and abilities to help themselves and to stand up to the Germans. But this particular group stood up, was all killed. And she asked in that letter not to ever forget and to try to make up for what they all have missed. I always tried, in the time that I'm happy, I try to remember those who are not happy. I heard once a very learned rabbi, Rabbi Lamm from New York, say each one of us who has survived has to live two lives, one, the life that's our life, and one, the life for somebody that was murdered. I do not know how to live two lives. But I tried to bring up my children, I tried to give them the right education that I understood as right, to give them an ability to be a part, a link, in the long, long chain of Judaism. I just hope, for generations to come, if somebody will see that CD-ROM, and somebody will look at that, should see it a part of an endeavor of the continuation of our people. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shmuel.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife shows what a lie it is that men are superior. After all my struggles and tribulations, I'm a lucky man. Lucky to survive and lucky to meet my wife. Nothing more to add to this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Rachel, on-- on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I want to thank you very, very much for sharing your story with us and allowing us to-- to-- to film this interview with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Thank you for taking the trouble, for being so nice and sensitive, and making it easier for us to give that interview. Thank Mr. Spielberg for founding the-- the-- the foundation and for doing all that. I hope it's going to be some help in the future. I hope so. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Testament forever, for [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
